name: Transmission of heritable information from generation to generation and the processes that increase genetic diversity

nodes.label: Meiosis & Gametogenesis
nodes.summary: Meiosis halves chromosome number from diploid to haploid, producing genetically unique gametes via two divisions.

nodes.label: Meiosis Overview & Comparison to Mitosis
nodes.summary: Meiosis is a reductional division (2n to n) with one round of DNA replication followed by two divisions.

nodes.label: Purpose of Meiosis
nodes.summary: Meiosis produces haploid gametes for sexual reproduction and generates genetic variation, maintaining constant chromosome number across generations.

nodes.label: Reductional vs Equational Division
nodes.summary: Meiosis I is reductional (separates homologs, 2n to n); Meiosis II is equational (separates sister chromatids).

nodes.label: Single S-phase, Two Divisions
nodes.summary: DNA replicates once before meiosis, then two consecutive divisions yield four haploid cells.

nodes.label: Four Haploid Daughter Cells
nodes.summary: Meiosis produces four genetically distinct haploid cells from one diploid parent.

nodes.label: Meiosis vs Mitosis Genetic Outcome
nodes.summary: Mitosis yields two identical diploid cells; meiosis yields four genetically diverse haploid cells.

nodes.label: Diploid (2n) vs Haploid (n)
nodes.summary: Diploid cells carry two sets of chromosomes (2n); haploid gametes carry one set (n); humans are 2n=46, n=23.

nodes.label: Homologous Chromosomes
nodes.summary: Homologs are paired chromosomes of the same length/centromere position carrying the same genes at the same loci, one maternal and one paternal.

nodes.label: Sister Chromatids
nodes.summary: Sister chromatids are identical copies of one chromosome joined at the centromere after replication.

nodes.label: Tetrad (Bivalent)
nodes.summary: A tetrad is a paired set of two homologous chromosomes (four chromatids) formed during synapsis in prophase I.

nodes.label: Centromere & Kinetochore in Meiosis
nodes.summary: In meiosis I, sister kinetochores attach to the same pole (co-orientation) so homologs separate; in meiosis II they attach to opposite poles like mitosis.

nodes.label: Meiosis I Stages
nodes.summary: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes through prophase I, metaphase I, anaphase I, and telophase I.

nodes.label: Prophase I
nodes.summary: In prophase I homologs synapse to form tetrads and crossing over occurs at chiasmata.

nodes.label: Synapsis & Synaptonemal Complex
nodes.summary: Synapsis is the pairing of homologs, held together by the protein synaptonemal complex.

nodes.label: Metaphase I
nodes.summary: Tetrads align at the metaphase plate, with homologs attached to spindle fibers from opposite poles.

nodes.label: Anaphase I & Disjunction
nodes.summary: Homologous chromosomes separate (disjunction) to opposite poles while sister chromatids stay together.

nodes.label: Telophase I & Cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Telophase I yields two haploid cells, each with chromosomes still composed of two sister chromatids.

nodes.label: Meiosis II Stages
nodes.summary: Meiosis II is a mitosis-like division that separates sister chromatids without prior DNA replication.

nodes.label: Interkinesis (No S-phase)
nodes.summary: Interkinesis is the brief interval between meiosis I and II during which no DNA replication occurs.

nodes.label: Prophase II
nodes.summary: Chromosomes recondense and a new spindle forms in each haploid cell; no crossing over occurs.

nodes.label: Metaphase II
nodes.summary: Individual chromosomes align singly at the metaphase plate, like in mitotic metaphase.

nodes.label: Anaphase II
nodes.summary: Sister chromatids separate to opposite poles in each cell.

nodes.label: Telophase II
nodes.summary: Four genetically distinct haploid daughter cells are produced.

nodes.label: Gametogenesis
nodes.summary: Gametogenesis is the production of gametes via meiosis, differing in males (spermatogenesis) and females (oogenesis).

nodes.label: Spermatogenesis
nodes.summary: Spermatogenesis produces four functional haploid sperm per primary spermatocyte, continuously from puberty.

nodes.label: Oogenesis
nodes.summary: Oogenesis produces one functional egg plus polar bodies, with arrests at prophase I and metaphase II.

nodes.label: Meiotic Arrest in Oogenesis
nodes.summary: Primary oocytes arrest in prophase I until ovulation; secondary oocytes arrest in metaphase II until fertilization.

nodes.label: Polar Bodies & Unequal Cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Unequal cytoplasmic division in oogenesis yields one large ovum and small nonfunctional polar bodies.

nodes.label: Fertilization Restores Diploidy
nodes.summary: Fusion of two haploid gametes at fertilization restores the diploid (2n) chromosome number.

nodes.label: Sources of Genetic Variation in Meiosis
nodes.summary: Independent assortment, crossing over, and random fertilization generate enormous genetic diversity.

nodes.label: Independent Assortment
nodes.summary: Random orientation of homolog pairs at metaphase I shuffles maternal and paternal chromosomes independently.

nodes.label: Random Metaphase I Orientation
nodes.summary: Each tetrad orients independently at metaphase I, so each pole receives a random mix of maternal and paternal homologs.

nodes.label: 2^n Gamete Combinations
nodes.summary: Independent assortment alone yields 2^n possible gamete chromosome combinations (2^23 in humans).

nodes.label: Crossing Over (Recombination)
nodes.summary: Crossing over exchanges segments between homologs in prophase I, producing recombinant chromosomes.

nodes.label: Chiasmata
nodes.summary: Chiasmata are the visible cross points where homologous nonsister chromatids exchange genetic material.

nodes.label: Recombinant Chromatids
nodes.summary: Crossing over creates chromatids with new combinations of maternal and paternal alleles.

nodes.label: Nonsister Chromatid Exchange
nodes.summary: Genetic exchange occurs between nonsister chromatids of homologous chromosomes, not between sisters.

nodes.label: Crossing Over Expands Diversity Beyond Assortment
nodes.summary: Crossing over creates novel allele combinations on a single chromosome, multiplying diversity beyond the 2^n from independent assortment.

nodes.label: Random Fertilization
nodes.summary: Random union of any sperm with any egg multiplies the diversity already produced by meiosis.

nodes.label: Zygote Combinations
nodes.summary: Random fertilization combined with independent assortment yields (2^n)^2 chromosome combinations per zygote, vastly more with crossing over.

nodes.label: Mendelian Genetics
nodes.summary: Mendel's laws describe how single genes with dominant and recessive alleles are transmitted across generations.

nodes.label: Mendel's Experimental Approach
nodes.summary: Mendel's pea-plant crosses established particulate (not blending) inheritance and the gene concept.

nodes.label: Mendel's Pea-Plant Crosses
nodes.summary: Mendel used controlled crosses of true-breeding pea plants with discrete traits to infer the rules of inheritance.

nodes.label: Particulate vs Blending Inheritance
nodes.summary: Traits are passed as discrete heritable units (genes), not blended, so recessive traits can reappear in later generations.

nodes.label: True-Breeding (Pure Line)
nodes.summary: A true-breeding line is homozygous for a trait and produces offspring identical to the parent when self-crossed.

nodes.label: Core Genetic Terminology
nodes.summary: Genes, alleles, loci, genotype and phenotype define the language of inheritance.

nodes.label: Gene
nodes.summary: A gene is a unit of heredity occupying a specific locus and encoding a trait.

nodes.label: Allele
nodes.summary: An allele is an alternative version of a gene at a given locus.

nodes.label: Locus
nodes.summary: A locus is the fixed physical position of a gene on a chromosome.

nodes.label: Genotype
nodes.summary: Genotype is the genetic (allelic) makeup of an organism at a locus.

nodes.label: Phenotype
nodes.summary: Phenotype is the observable trait resulting from genotype and environment.

nodes.label: Homozygous
nodes.summary: Homozygous means having two identical alleles at a locus.

nodes.label: Heterozygous
nodes.summary: Heterozygous means having two different alleles at a locus.

nodes.label: Hemizygous
nodes.summary: Hemizygous describes having only one allele for a gene, as for X-linked genes in males.

nodes.label: Dominant Allele
nodes.summary: A dominant allele is fully expressed in the phenotype when present in at least one copy.

nodes.label: Recessive Allele
nodes.summary: A recessive allele is expressed phenotypically only when homozygous.

nodes.label: Complete Dominance
nodes.summary: In complete dominance the heterozygote phenotype is identical to the homozygous dominant phenotype.

nodes.label: Wild-Type vs Mutant
nodes.summary: Wild type is the most common naturally occurring allele/phenotype; mutant alleles arise by mutation.

nodes.label: Mendel's Laws
nodes.summary: Mendel derived the laws of segregation and independent assortment plus the concept of dominance.

nodes.label: Law of Segregation
nodes.summary: Each individual carries two alleles per gene that separate during gamete formation so each gamete gets one.

nodes.label: Law of Independent Assortment
nodes.summary: Alleles of different genes assort independently into gametes when genes are on different chromosomes.

nodes.label: Law (Concept) of Dominance
nodes.summary: In a heterozygote, the dominant allele masks the recessive allele in the phenotype.

nodes.label: Genetic Crosses & Punnett Squares
nodes.summary: Punnett squares predict genotypic and phenotypic ratios of offspring from parental crosses.

nodes.label: Punnett Square Method
nodes.summary: A Punnett square arrays parental gamete genotypes to enumerate all possible offspring genotype combinations and their probabilities.

nodes.label: P, F1, F2 Generations
nodes.summary: The parental (P), first filial (F1), and second filial (F2) generations track inheritance across crosses.

nodes.label: Monohybrid Cross & 3:1 Ratio
nodes.summary: A heterozygous monohybrid cross (Aa x Aa) yields a 3:1 dominant-to-recessive phenotypic ratio and 1:2:1 genotype ratio.

nodes.label: Dihybrid Cross & 9:3:3:1 Ratio
nodes.summary: A dihybrid cross of two heterozygotes (AaBb x AaBb) yields a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio.

nodes.label: Test Cross
nodes.summary: Crossing an unknown dominant phenotype with a homozygous recessive reveals the unknown genotype.

nodes.label: Backcross
nodes.summary: A backcross mates an offspring with one of its parents (or a genotype identical to a parent).

nodes.label: Reciprocal Cross
nodes.summary: Reciprocal crosses swap the sexes of the parental phenotypes to detect sex-linked or cytoplasmic inheritance.

nodes.label: Probability in Genetics
nodes.summary: The rules of multiplication and addition compute combined inheritance probabilities.

nodes.label: Product Rule (AND)
nodes.summary: The probability of independent events both occurring is the product of their individual probabilities.

nodes.label: Sum Rule (OR)
nodes.summary: The probability of either of two mutually exclusive events is the sum of their probabilities.

nodes.label: Probability Method for Multi-Gene Crosses
nodes.summary: Solve complex multi-gene crosses by treating each gene independently and multiplying single-gene probabilities instead of a large Punnett square.

nodes.label: Non-Mendelian & Complex Inheritance
nodes.summary: Many traits deviate from simple dominance, including codominance, linkage, sex linkage, and polygenic inheritance.

nodes.label: Variations on Dominance
nodes.summary: Incomplete dominance, codominance, and multiple alleles modify simple dominant/recessive expression.

nodes.label: Incomplete Dominance
nodes.summary: Heterozygotes show an intermediate (blended) phenotype, as in pink snapdragons.

nodes.label: Codominance
nodes.summary: Both alleles are fully and simultaneously expressed in the heterozygote, as in AB blood type.

nodes.label: Multiple Alleles
nodes.summary: A gene may have more than two alleles in a population, though an individual carries only two.

nodes.label: ABO Blood Group System
nodes.summary: The ABO system combines multiple alleles (IA, IB, i) with codominance of IA/IB and recessive i.

nodes.label: Rh Blood Group
nodes.summary: The Rh (D) antigen is inherited as a simple dominant; Rh+ x Rh- crosses underlie hemolytic disease of the newborn.

nodes.label: Lethal Alleles
nodes.summary: Alleles lethal when homozygous distort expected ratios (e.g., a 3:1 becomes 2:1 among survivors).

nodes.label: Pleiotropy
nodes.summary: A single gene affects multiple phenotypic traits, as in sickle cell disease.

nodes.label: Penetrance & Expressivity
nodes.summary: Penetrance is the fraction of genotypes showing the phenotype; expressivity is the degree of expression.

nodes.label: Gene Interactions
nodes.summary: Epistasis and polygenic inheritance involve multiple genes shaping a phenotype.

nodes.label: Epistasis
nodes.summary: One gene masks or modifies the phenotypic expression of another gene at a different locus.

nodes.label: Bombay Phenotype (Recessive Epistasis)
nodes.summary: Homozygous recessive at the H locus prevents ABO antigen display, masking the ABO genotype as type O.

nodes.label: Modified Dihybrid Ratios
nodes.summary: Epistasis converts the 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio into variants such as 9:3:4, 12:3:1, or 9:7.

nodes.label: Polygenic Inheritance
nodes.summary: Many genes contribute additively to a single quantitative trait, producing continuous variation.

nodes.label: Continuous (Quantitative) Variation
nodes.summary: Polygenic and environmental effects produce a bell-shaped distribution of phenotypes like height.

nodes.label: Environmental Influence on Phenotype
nodes.summary: The environment can modify gene expression and phenotype (gene-environment interaction).

nodes.label: Genetic Linkage & Mapping
nodes.summary: Genes near each other on a chromosome are inherited together, deviating from independent assortment.

nodes.label: Linked Genes
nodes.summary: Genes on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together and violate independent assortment.

nodes.label: Parental vs Recombinant Types
nodes.summary: Linked-gene crosses yield abundant parental-type offspring and fewer recombinant types produced by crossing over.

nodes.label: Recombination Frequency
nodes.summary: Recombination frequency = recombinant offspring / total offspring, increasing with distance between genes.

nodes.label: Map Units (Centimorgans)
nodes.summary: 1 map unit (centimorgan) equals a 1 percent recombination frequency, used to build genetic maps.

nodes.label: Building Linkage Maps
nodes.summary: Recombination frequencies between gene pairs are summed to order genes and estimate distances on a chromosome.

nodes.label: 50% Recombination Ceiling
nodes.summary: Genes far apart or on different chromosomes show a maximum ~50 percent recombination, appearing unlinked.

nodes.label: Sex Determination & Sex Linkage
nodes.summary: Sex chromosomes determine sex and carry sex-linked genes with characteristic inheritance patterns.

nodes.label: XX/XY Sex Determination
nodes.summary: In humans, XX is female and XY is male, with the SRY gene on Y triggering male development.

nodes.label: Autosomes vs Sex Chromosomes
nodes.summary: 22 autosome pairs carry non-sex genes while the 23rd pair (sex chromosomes) determines sex.

nodes.label: X-Linked Inheritance
nodes.summary: X-linked recessive traits appear more often in males who are hemizygous for the X chromosome.

nodes.label: Criss-Cross Inheritance
nodes.summary: X-linked recessive traits pass from affected fathers to carrier daughters to affected grandsons.

nodes.label: X-Linked Recessive Disease Examples
nodes.summary: Hemophilia, red-green color blindness, and Duchenne muscular dystrophy are classic X-linked recessive conditions.

nodes.label: Y-Linked (Holandric) Inheritance
nodes.summary: Y-linked genes pass strictly from father to all sons.

nodes.label: X-Inactivation (Barr Body)
nodes.summary: Dosage compensation randomly inactivates one X in females, forming a Barr body and causing mosaicism.

nodes.label: Mosaicism (Calico Cats)
nodes.summary: Random X-inactivation in heterozygous females yields patches of differing gene expression, as in calico cat coat color.

nodes.label: Sex-Linked vs Sex-Influenced/Limited
nodes.summary: Traits can be sex-linked (on sex chromosomes) or sex-influenced/sex-limited (autosomal but sex-dependent expression).

nodes.label: Sex-Influenced Traits
nodes.summary: Autosomal traits expressed differently in the two sexes due to hormones, as in pattern baldness.

nodes.label: Sex-Limited Traits
nodes.summary: Autosomal genes expressed in only one sex, such as milk production in females.

nodes.label: Extranuclear & Epigenetic Inheritance
nodes.summary: Some inheritance bypasses nuclear Mendelian rules via organelle DNA, imprinting, and chromatin modification.

nodes.label: Cytoplasmic (Mitochondrial) Inheritance
nodes.summary: Organelle DNA is inherited from the cytoplasm, typically maternally, outside Mendelian segregation.

nodes.label: Organelle (Mitochondrial/Chloroplast) DNA
nodes.summary: Mitochondria and chloroplasts carry their own circular DNA passed through the cytoplasm.

nodes.label: Maternal (Mitochondrial) Inheritance
nodes.summary: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited almost exclusively from the mother via the egg cytoplasm.

nodes.label: Heteroplasmy
nodes.summary: Cells may carry a mixture of normal and mutant mitochondrial DNA, so disease severity depends on the mutant proportion.

nodes.label: Epigenetic Inheritance
nodes.summary: Heritable changes in gene expression without DNA sequence change, via methylation and chromatin marks.

nodes.label: Epigenetics Defined
nodes.summary: Epigenetics is heritable change in gene expression that does not alter the underlying DNA sequence.

nodes.label: DNA Methylation
nodes.summary: Heritable methylation of cytosines silences genes without altering the underlying DNA sequence.

nodes.label: Histone Modification
nodes.summary: Heritable acetylation/methylation of histones alters chromatin accessibility and gene expression.

nodes.label: Genomic Imprinting
nodes.summary: Some genes are expressed only from the maternal or only the paternal allele due to parent-of-origin marks.

nodes.label: Chromosomal Basis & Mutations
nodes.summary: The chromosome theory of inheritance and chromosomal/genetic mutations underlie transmission and variation.

nodes.label: Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance
nodes.summary: Genes reside on chromosomes whose meiotic behavior explains Mendel's laws.

nodes.label: Chromosome Theory Statement
nodes.summary: Sutton and Boveri proposed that Mendelian factors are carried on chromosomes, linking genes to chromosome behavior.

nodes.label: Karyotype
nodes.summary: A karyotype is the organized display of an individual's full chromosome set used to detect abnormalities.

nodes.label: Aneuploidy & Nondisjunction
nodes.summary: Errors in chromosome separation produce abnormal chromosome numbers that disrupt inheritance.

nodes.label: Nondisjunction
nodes.summary: Failure of chromosomes/chromatids to separate in meiosis produces gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers.

nodes.label: Euploidy vs Aneuploidy
nodes.summary: Euploidy is a complete chromosome-set multiple; aneuploidy is the gain or loss of individual chromosomes.

nodes.label: Monosomy & Trisomy
nodes.summary: Aneuploidy yields monosomy (2n-1) or trisomy (2n+1), as in trisomy 21 (Down syndrome).

nodes.label: Autosomal Aneuploidy Syndromes
nodes.summary: Trisomy 21 (Down), trisomy 18 (Edwards), and trisomy 13 (Patau) are viable autosomal trisomies.

nodes.label: Sex-Chromosome Aneuploidies
nodes.summary: Turner syndrome (XO monosomy) and Klinefelter syndrome (XXY trisomy) result from sex-chromosome nondisjunction.

nodes.label: Polyploidy
nodes.summary: Polyploidy is having more than two complete chromosome sets, common in plants and lethal in most animals.

nodes.label: Chromosomal Structural Aberrations
nodes.summary: Breakage and rejoining of chromosomes cause deletions, duplications, inversions, and translocations.

nodes.label: Deletion
nodes.summary: A deletion removes a chromosomal segment, losing the genes it contained.

nodes.label: Duplication
nodes.summary: A duplication repeats a chromosomal segment, increasing gene copy number.

nodes.label: Inversion
nodes.summary: An inversion reverses the orientation of a chromosomal segment.

nodes.label: Translocation
nodes.summary: A translocation moves a chromosomal segment to a nonhomologous chromosome.

nodes.label: Robertsonian Translocation
nodes.summary: Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at the centromere can cause familial Down syndrome.

nodes.label: Gene Mutations as a Variation Source
nodes.summary: Heritable DNA sequence changes are the ultimate source of new alleles and genetic variation.

nodes.label: Point Mutation
nodes.summary: A point mutation changes a single nucleotide and may be silent, missense, or nonsense.

nodes.label: Silent, Missense & Nonsense Mutations
nodes.summary: A substitution is silent (no amino-acid change), missense (different amino acid), or nonsense (premature stop codon).

nodes.label: Transitions vs Transversions
nodes.summary: A transition swaps a purine for a purine (or pyrimidine for pyrimidine); a transversion swaps a purine for a pyrimidine.

nodes.label: Frameshift (Insertion/Deletion)
nodes.summary: Insertions or deletions not in multiples of three shift the reading frame downstream.

nodes.label: Germline vs Somatic Mutation
nodes.summary: Only germline mutations are heritable; somatic mutations affect the individual but are not passed on.

nodes.label: Spontaneous vs Induced Mutation
nodes.summary: Spontaneous mutations arise from replication errors/tautomeric shifts; induced mutations are caused by mutagens.

nodes.label: Mutagens
nodes.summary: Chemical and radiation mutagens increase mutation rate and thus heritable variation.

nodes.label: Mutation as the Origin of New Alleles
nodes.summary: Mutation is the ultimate source of all new alleles and thus all heritable genetic variation in a population.

nodes.label: Pedigree Analysis
nodes.summary: Pedigrees trace inheritance through family trees to deduce trait inheritance patterns and carrier status.

nodes.label: Pedigree Symbols & Conventions
nodes.summary: Standardized symbols depict sex, affected status, mating, and generations in a family tree.

nodes.label: Pedigree Symbols
nodes.summary: Squares represent males, circles females, shaded shapes affected individuals, and lines matings/offspring.

nodes.label: Carrier
nodes.summary: A carrier is a heterozygote who is phenotypically unaffected but can transmit a recessive allele.

nodes.label: Proband & Generation Numbering
nodes.summary: The proband is the index individual through whom a pedigree is ascertained; generations are numbered with Roman numerals.

nodes.label: Identifying Inheritance Patterns
nodes.summary: Pedigree clues distinguish autosomal vs X-linked and dominant vs recessive inheritance.

nodes.label: Autosomal Recessive Pattern
nodes.summary: Affected children often have unaffected carrier parents; trait can skip generations and affects sexes equally.

nodes.label: Autosomal Dominant Pattern
nodes.summary: Trait appears every generation, affects both sexes, and each affected child has an affected parent.

nodes.label: X-Linked Recessive Pattern
nodes.summary: More affected males than females, no male-to-male transmission, often through carrier mothers.

nodes.label: X-Linked Dominant Pattern
nodes.summary: Affected fathers transmit to all daughters but no sons; affects both sexes with female bias.

nodes.label: Mitochondrial Pedigree Pattern
nodes.summary: Affected mothers transmit to all children; affected fathers transmit to none.

nodes.label: Male-to-Male Transmission Rule
nodes.summary: Father-to-son transmission rules out X-linked inheritance because sons inherit the father's Y, not his X.

nodes.label: Computing Risk from a Pedigree
nodes.summary: Carrier probabilities and offspring risk are computed by combining genotype inferences with the product and sum rules.

nodes.label: Population Genetics & Hardy-Weinberg
nodes.summary: Population genetics models allele and genotype frequencies and how they change to drive evolution.

nodes.label: Gene Pool & Allele Frequencies
nodes.summary: A gene pool is all alleles in a population; their frequencies define its genetic structure.

nodes.label: Gene Pool
nodes.summary: The gene pool is the total collection of alleles for all genes in a population.

nodes.label: Allele & Genotype Frequencies
nodes.summary: Allele frequency is the proportion of a given allele in the gene pool, summing to 1.

nodes.label: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
nodes.summary: In an idealized non-evolving population, allele and genotype frequencies stay constant across generations.

nodes.label: Allele Frequency Equation (p + q = 1)
nodes.summary: For two alleles, p + q = 1 where p and q are the dominant and recessive allele frequencies.

nodes.label: Genotype Equation (p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1)
nodes.summary: Genotype frequencies follow p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1 for AA, Aa, and aa respectively.

nodes.label: Interpreting the H-W Terms
nodes.summary: p^2 is homozygous dominant frequency, 2pq heterozygous (carrier) frequency, and q^2 homozygous recessive frequency.

nodes.label: Hardy-Weinberg Assumptions
nodes.summary: Equilibrium requires no mutation, no migration, no selection, random mating, and a large population.

nodes.label: Solving H-W Problems
nodes.summary: Given the recessive phenotype frequency (q^2), solve for q, then p, then carrier frequency (2pq).

nodes.label: Forces That Change Allele Frequencies
nodes.summary: Mutation, gene flow, genetic drift, nonrandom mating, and selection disrupt Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

nodes.label: Microevolution & Allele-Frequency Change
nodes.summary: Microevolution is a change in allele frequencies within a population over generations, the basis of evolution.

nodes.label: Mutation as Evolutionary Force
nodes.summary: Mutation introduces new alleles, slowly changing allele frequencies and being the ultimate source of variation.

nodes.label: Gene Flow (Migration)
nodes.summary: Movement of alleles between populations via migration alters allele frequencies and increases diversity.

nodes.label: Genetic Drift
nodes.summary: Random change in allele frequencies, strongest in small populations.

nodes.label: Bottleneck Effect
nodes.summary: A drastic population reduction randomly shrinks genetic diversity.

nodes.label: Founder Effect
nodes.summary: A new population started by few individuals carries only a nonrandom subset of the original gene pool.

nodes.label: Nonrandom Mating & Inbreeding
nodes.summary: Assortative mating and inbreeding shift genotype frequencies, increasing homozygosity.

nodes.label: Natural Selection
nodes.summary: Differential reproductive success based on fitness changes allele frequencies adaptively.

nodes.label: Fitness
nodes.summary: Fitness is the relative reproductive success of a genotype, determining which alleles increase in frequency.

nodes.label: Directional, Stabilizing & Disruptive Selection
nodes.summary: Directional selection favors one extreme, stabilizing favors the mean, and disruptive favors both extremes.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Mitosis produces identical diploid daughter cells
Mitotic spindle apparatus and centromere/kinetochore attachment
Gene as a DNA sequence encoding a product (central dogma)
Genetic code, codons, and translation reading frame
Codon degeneracy, stop codons, and translation
Reading frame and codon triplet structure
Homologous recombination / DNA strand exchange mechanism
DNA structure and regulation of gene expression (chromatin)
Glycosyltransferases adding antigen sugars (gene product to phenotype)
Probability rules (independent and mutually exclusive events)
